back: A set is countable if it is finite or if it can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers (i.e., it has the same cardinality as ℕ).

A set is uncountable if it is infinite and cannot be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers. Its cardinality is strictly greater than that of ℕ, such as the cardinality of the real numbers (ℝ).

front: Is the set of all rational numbers (ℚ) countable or uncountable? Prove it.
back: The set of rational numbers (ℚ) is countable. This can be proven by arranging the rationals in a two-dimensional grid (by numerator and denominator) and using a diagonal counting argument to list them in a sequence, establishing a bijection with ℕ.

front: What is Cantor's Diagonalization Argument, and what does it prove?
back: Cantor's Diagonalization Argument is a proof technique that demonstrates the set of real numbers (or the set of all infinite sequences of bits) is uncountable. It shows that any attempted list of all real numbers in [0,1] will always miss at least one number, constructed by changing the nth digit of the nth number in the list.

front: What is the cardinality of the power set of a countable set?
back: The power set (the set of all subsets) of any countable infinite set is uncountable. Its cardinality is 2^(ℵ₀), which is the cardinality of the continuum (the same as the real numbers ℝ).
